title: Chapter 6: Energy, Work and Power — Hard

questions.question: A $2.0\text{ kg}$ flower pot falls from a height of $45\text{ m}$ in a vacuum. Calculate its speed just before it hits the ground. ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
questions.explanation:
Initial $GPE = mgh = 2 \times 10 \times 45 = 900\text{ J}$. This converts to $KE$: $900 = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 rightarrow 900 = 0.5 \times 2 \times v^2 rightarrow v^2 = 900 rightarrow v = 30\text{ m/s}$. 

**Common mistake:** Providing the value of $v^2$ ($900$) as the speed.

questions.question: A bullet of mass $20\text{ g}$ travels at a speed of $1200\text{ m/s}$. Calculate its kinetic energy.
questions.explanation:
First, convert mass to kg: $20\text{ g} = 0.02\text{ kg}$. Then $E_k = 0.5 \times 0.02 \times 1200^2 = 0.01 \times 1 440 000 = 14 400\text{ J}$. 

**Common mistake:** Using mass in grams ($20$) directly in the formula, leading to an answer^1000 \times too large.

questions.question: A roller coaster descends from $30\text{ m}$ to $10\text{ m}$ above the ground. If $20\%$ of the gravitational potential energy lost is dissipated as heat and sound, what happens to the remaining $80\%$?
options: It is converted into kinetic energy
It is destroyed according to the efficiency law
It remains as gravitational potential energy
It is converted into chemical energy
questions.explanation:
The principle of conservation of energy states total energy is constant. If $20\%$ is wasted, the rest ($80\%$) of the lost potential energy must convert into kinetic energy. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the $80\%$ refers to the total energy at the start rather than just the lost portion.

questions.question: An ideal pendulum in a vacuum is displaced and released. Why will it swing forever?
options: The total amount of energy is conserved and no energy is dissipated
Gravitational potential energy is continuously created
Kinetic energy increases every time it reaches the midpoint
The force of gravity is zero at the bottom of the swing
questions.explanation:
In an 'ideal' system in a vacuum, there is no air resistance or friction. Energy only converts between GPE and KE without being dissipated to the surroundings. 

**Common mistake:** Believing energy is being 'added' to keep it moving.

questions.question: A robot waiter uses electrical energy to move food at a constant height. One guest says 'The electrical energy is used up as the robot moves'. Why is this explanation scientifically incorrect?
options: Energy cannot be used up; it must be converted to other forms
The robot is not doing any work if the height is constant
The electrical energy is converted into extra gravitational potential energy
Electrical energy is only used to power the flashing lights
questions.explanation:
According to the principle of conservation of energy, energy is never 'used up' or destroyed; it is converted (e.g., to KE or thermal energy). 

**Common mistake:** Using the everyday term 'using up energy' in a strict scientific context.

questions.question: A $1.5\text{ kg}$ model car is pulled $1.2\text{ m}$ up a slope by a $10\text{ N}$ force parallel to the slope. If the vertical height gained is $0.6\text{ m}$, calculate the work done by the $10\text{ N}$ force.
questions.explanation: The work done by the specific $10\text{ N}$ force is $F \times \text{distance along the slope} = 10\text{ N} \times 1.2\text{ m} = 12\text{ J}$. **Common mistake:** Calculating the potential energy gain ($1.5 \times 10 \times 0.6 = 9\text{ J}$) and identifying it as the work done by the pulling force.

questions.question: A student carries books weighing $20\text{ N}$ while walking at a constant speed across a horizontal floor for $10\text{ m}$. How much work is done by the **upward** force $F$ the student exerts on the books?
questions.explanation: Work is force \times distance moved **in the direction of the force**. The force $F$ is upward, but the distance moved is horizontal ($90^{circ}$ away). Thus, the distance moved in the direction of $F$ is zero. **Common mistake:** Simply multiplying $20\text{ N} \times 10\text{ m}$ to get $200\text{ J}$.

questions.question: An object of mass $2\text{ kg}$ is initially at rest. A resultant force does $100\text{ J}$ of work on it. Assuming no energy is dissipated, calculate its final speed.
questions.explanation: Work done is transferred to kinetic energy ($E_k$): $100\text{ J} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$. $100 = 0.5 \times 2 \times v^2 Rightarrow v^2 = 100 Rightarrow v = 10\text{ m/s}$. **Common mistake:** Thinking the work done is equal to the speed directly ($100$) or ignoring the $1/2$ in the formula.

questions.question: A machine lifts $200\text{ kg}$ of bricks vertically through $30\text{ m}$ in $50\text{ s}$. How much mechanical work is performed by the machine? (Take $g = 10\text{ N/kg}$)
questions.explanation: Work $= F \times s = (mg) \times h = 200 \times 10 \times 30 = 60000\text{ J}$. **Common mistake:** Including the time ($50\text{ s}$) in the calculation, which results in power ($1200\text{ W}$) rather than work.

questions.question: A person pulls a box with a force $F$. If the work done is transferred into both kinetic energy and thermal energy (due to friction), which statement is correct?
options: The principle of conservation of energy does not apply here
questions.explanation: The total work done by the applied force is the total energy transferred, which is divided between the increase in kinetic energy and the energy dissipated as heat. **Common mistake:** Thinking work only relates to the 'useful' energy ($E_k$) and ignoring friction.

questions.question: A hoist with $60\%$ efficiency is used to lift a $20\text{ kg}$ crate vertically through $3\text{ m}$. Calculate the total electrical energy input required. (Take $g = 10\text{ N/kg}$)
questions.explanation:
Useful work = $mgh = 20 \times 10 \times 3 = 600\text{ J}$. Energy Input = $Useful / \text{Efficiency} = 600 / 0.6 = 1000\text{ J}$. 

**Common mistake:** Multiplying^600 by $0.6$ ($360$) instead of dividing.

questions.question: A Sankey diagram for a light bulb shows an input of $100\text{ J}$, with $10\text{ J}$ pointing right as light and a large arrow pointing down. What does the downward arrow represent?
options: The efficiency of the light bulb
questions.explanation:
In a Sankey diagram, the useful output points right and wasted energy points down. Wasted = $100 - 10 = 90\text{ J}$. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the downward arrow is the 'useful' part.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the current state of research regarding nuclear fusion on Earth?
options: Scientists are investigating how to use it to produce electrical energy on a large scale.
It is the main process used in all modern commercial power stations.
It has been abandoned because it produces too much carbon dioxide.
It is only possible to perform fusion in a vacuum flask.
questions.explanation:
Research is being carried out to investigate how fusion can be used for large-scale electrical energy. 

**Common mistake:** Believing fusion is already the standard method for electricity.

questions.question: Based on the principle of conservation of energy, why does a roller coaster not continue its motion forever?
options: Energy is dissipated as thermal energy due to friction and air resistance.
Gravitational potential energy is gradually destroyed as the coaster falls.
The total amount of energy in the system decreases over time.
The Sun's energy is not strong enough to maintain the motion.
questions.explanation:
Energy is conserved but is converted into less useful forms like heat (dissipated). It is not 'destroyed'. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking energy is used up or destroyed during the ride.

questions.question: Which set of energy resources consists entirely of those that do NOT depend on the Sun?
questions.explanation:
According to the syllabus notes, the Sun is the source for all except geothermal, nuclear, and tidal resources. 

**Common mistake:** Including tidal or nuclear in Sun-dependent lists.

questions.question: Eugene, who weighs $450\text{ N}$, runs up ten steps. Each step is $0.20\text{ m}$ high. If he takes five seconds to reach the top, what is his power output?
questions.explanation:
Total height $h = 10 \times 0.20 = 2.0\text{ m}$. Work $W = F \times h = 450 \times 2.0 = 900\text{ J}$. Power $P = 900 / 5 = 180\text{ W}$. 

**Common mistake:** Using only the height of a single step ($0.20\text{ m}$) in the calculation ($18\text{ W}$).

questions.question: A $40\text{ W}$ filament lamp converts $10\%$ of its electrical energy into light energy. How much light energy is produced in five minutes?
questions.explanation:
Total energy $E = P \times t = 40\text{ W} \times (5 \times 60)\text{ s} = 12000\text{ J}$. Light energy $= 10\% \text{ of } 12000 = 1200\text{ J} = 1.2\text{ kJ}$. 

**Common mistake:** Forgetting to convert minutes to seconds or failing to apply the $10\%$ efficiency factor.

questions.question: Data for a power station shows a daily useful energy output of $7.5 \times 10^{13}\text{ J}$. Calculate the average power generated by the station in megawatts ($MW$).
questions.explanation:
Time in one day $= 24 \times 3600 = 86400\text{ s}$. Power $= (7.5 \times 10^{13}) / 86400 \approx 868055556\text{ W} \approx 868\text{ MW}$. 

**Common mistake:** Dividing by^24 (hours) only rather than seconds.

questions.question: A machine with an efficiency of $25\%$ is used to lift a $200\text{ kg}$ load to a height of $30\text{ m}$. If the total power input is $24\text{ kW}$, how long does the lift take? (Take $g = 10\text{ N/kg}$)
questions.explanation:
Useful Power $= 25\% \text{ of } 24000\text{ W} = 6000\text{ W}$. Useful Work $W = mgh = 200 \times 10 \times 30 = 60000\text{ J}$. Time $t = W / P_{useful} = 60000 / 6000 = 10\text{ s}$. 

**Common mistake:** Using the total input power ($24\text{ kW}$) instead of the useful power output.

questions.question: A toy wheel is launched by pushing an adapter vertically upwards along a rod. In Experiment A, the full length of the rod is used. In Experiment B, only half the length is used. If the adapter is pushed with the same force over the same time in both, which statement is correct?
options: The launching power in A is twice that of B
The launching power in B is twice that of A
The launching power is the same in both
No work is done in either case
questions.explanation:
In A, the distance $s  is doubled compared to B. Since Work = Fs , Work in A is double. Because Power = Work / time$ and time is the same, Power in A is double. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking power only depends on the force applied regardless of distance.
